quoteFirst: —We have been in three different towns today: Dar-es-Salaam, Zanzibar, and Tanga. We left Dar-es-Salaam at daylight, and stopped at Zanzibar for the mails at
quoteLast: , remaining an hour. Several of our friends came on board, but none of the passengers went ashore.
title: Travel letters from New Zealand, Australia and Africa
author: Howe, E. W. (Edgar Watson), 1853-1937

quoteLast: we were _en route_ and, worried by swarms of flies, in forty minutes we walked up the great ugly prism, Reynivallaháls, whose winding way was hardly visible from below. The summit is dotted with Vörður, to guide travellers and church-goers through the snow.
title: Ultima Thule; or, A Summer in Iceland. vol. 2/2
author: Burton, Richard Francis, Sir, 1821-1890

quoteLast: of Monday, September 7,--Withers places it a month, less a day, too early,--the hostiles crossed the Kentucky a mile and a half above Boonesborough, at a point since known as Black Fish's Ford, and soon made their appearance marching single file, some of them mounted, along the ridge south of the fort.
title: Chronicles of border warfare
author: Withers, Alexander Scott, 1792-1865; Draper, Lyman Copeland, 1815-1891 [Contributor]; Thwaites, Reuben Gold, 1853-1913 [Editor]

quoteFirst: At Handeck, for the sake of the horse, and of Jean Ott, a halt of an hour was called. It was now
quoteLast: ; and so far, with a few short breaks, we had carried along with us the morning shade of the eastern mountains. Some bread and cheese, and a bottle of wine which, if not good in itself, was made to appear so by a five hours’ brisk walk in the fresh morning air, and by the addition of ice-cold water from the glacier that on the west overhangs the _châlet_, occupied, within the _châlet_, the first half of our time.
title: Swiss allmends, and a walk to see them
author: Zincke, F. Barham (Foster Barham), 1817-1893

quoteFirst: If there are more ships than one, they may anchor farther out in deeper water. During the night it was calm, and the weather became very foggy; but
quoteLast: it cleared up, and I went on shore. I found abundance of shell-fish, but saw no traces of people.

quoteFirst: He knew I was going to meet General Johnston, and volunteered to say that he hoped I would succeed in obtaining his surrender, as the whole army dreaded the long march to Charlotte (one hundred and seventy-five miles), already begun, but which had been interrupted by the receipt of General Johnston's letter of the 13th.  We reached Durham's, twenty-six miles,
quoteLast: , where General Kilpatrick had a squadron of cavalry drawn up to receive me.  We passed into the house in which he had his headquarters, and soon after mounted some led horses, which he had prepared for myself and staff.
title: Memoirs of General William T. Sherman — Volume 2
author: Sherman, William T. (William Tecumseh), 1820-1891

quoteLast: , a terrible crime was committed within four miles of Wickliffe, Ky. Two girls, Mary and Ruby Ray, were found murdered a short distance from their home.
title:

author: Wells-Barnett, Ida B., 1862-1931

quoteLast: the French infantry by a brilliant charge captured three lines of German trenches. The fortress of Douaumont was penetrated, and during the entire night a fierce struggle was continued within its walls.
